statement.markdown: Given an array of integers representing the size of each box, determine the minimum number of crates needed to store all boxes without any overflow. A crate can hold at most 3 boxes. The goal is to minimize the total number of crates used.
statement.inputFormat: Array of integers representing box sizes
statement.notes: Time and space complexity must be optimal (O(n) time, O(1) space).

hints.text: Consider using a greedy approach to fill crates as much as possible before creating a new one.

editorial.title: Optimal Crate Filling
editorial.intuition: The key to this problem is to find a way to fill crates as much as possible before creating new ones.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution involves using a greedy approach to fill crates until there are not enough elements to fill a new crate.
commonMistakes: Failing to consider the optimal crate filling strategy

buggyStarterCode.cpp: int solve(std::vector<int>& boxes) { int crates = 0; for (int i = 0; i < boxes.size(); i++) { crates++; if (boxes[i] > 3) crates++; } return crates; }
buggyStarterCode.java: public int solve(int[] boxes) { int crates = 0; for (int i = 0; i < boxes.length; i++) { crates++; if (boxes[i] > 3) crates++; } return crates; }
buggyStarterCode.python: def solve(self, boxes): crates = 0 for i in range(len(boxes)): crates++; if boxes[i] > 3: crates++; return crates
buggyStarterCode.javascript: var solve = function(boxes) { var crates = 0; for (var i = 0; i < boxes.length; i++) { crates++; if (boxes[i] > 3) crates++; } return crates; }

solutions.python:
class Solution:
    def solve(self, boxes):
        crates = 1
        current_box = 0
        for box in boxes:
            if box + current_box > 3:
                crates += 1
                current_box = 0
            current_box += box
        return crates

# Test cases
import sys
input = sys.stdin.read().splitlines()
solution = Solution()
boxes = list(map(int, input[1].split()))
print(solution.solve(boxes))
